ca
Abstract
Automated Fact-Checking (AFC) is the auto-
mated verification of claim accuracy
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
AFC is
crucial in discerning truth from misinformation,
especially given the huge amounts of content
are generated online daily
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Current research
focuses on predicting claim veracity through
metadata analysis and language scrutiny, with
an emphasis on justifying verdicts
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
This pa-
per surveys recent methodologies, proposing
a comprehensive taxonomy and presenting the
evolution of research in that landscape
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
A com-
parative analysis of methodologies and future
directions for improving fact-checking explain-
ability are also discussed
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
1
Introduction
The huge increase in both user-generated and au-
tomated content has led to a significant amount of
misinformation
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
This poses risks to uninformed
readers, highlighting the need for scalable, auto-
mated methods for verification and fact-checking
(Nakov et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
While predicting the verac-
ity of claims is essential, relying solely on pre-
dictions without providing explanations can be
counterproductive, potentially reinforcing belief
in false claims and perpetuating misinformation
(Lewandowsky et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Most fact-checking models use neural architec-
tures, but interpreting these models is challenging
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
There is a need for fact-checking frameworks pro-
viding justifications to enhance effectiveness and
trustworthiness
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
This survey presents recent ef-
forts addressing automatic justification production
for claim verification, emphasizing the move to-
wards “Explainable” Automated Fact-Checking
(AFC)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Some work refers to the justification pro-
duction process as the explanation generation pro-
cess (Kotonya and Toni, 2020a)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In this survey, the
term “justification production” is used following
the work of Guo et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
This survey’s main contribution is as follows:
Firstly, it introduces a multidimensional taxonomy
for categorizing works based on various criteria
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Secondly, it provides how research is progressing
towards standard justifications
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Thirdly, it conducts
a comparative analysis of justification production
approaches, pipeline architectures, input and out-
put types
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Lastly, it identifies challenges while
proposing future directions in justification produc-
tion
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Appendix A outlines the methodology utilized
for literature compilation, detailing the search strat-
egy and selection criteria employed for the papers
that form the cornerstone of this survey
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
2
Related Surveys
Thorne and Vlachos (2018) provided a comprehen-
sive review of early developments in fact-checking,
but they don’t focus on verdicts with justifications
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
(2022); Vladika and Matthes (2023) offer
broad overviews of the entire fact-checking pro-
cess and its various components
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In contrast, our
work specifically concentrates on the aspect of justi-
fication production
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Moreover, recent multi-modal
fact-checking surveys (Alam et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
, 2022; Vladika
and Matthes, 2023) mention that natural language
justification production remains unexplored in the
multi-modal AFC domain
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In this survey, we high-
light some of the emergent works in multi-modal
justification production
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The survey by Kotonya and Toni (2020a), focus-
ing on justification production, is closely related
to ours
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
However, since then, there has been a sig-
nificant progress driven by the rapid development
of transformer-based architectures and Large Lan-
guage Models (LLMs)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
(2023) only
augmented the latter work’s taxonomy with coun-
terfactual justifications
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
While partially covering
some recent work, they do not provide a compre-
hensive, new, detailed multi-dimensional taxonomy
as proposed in this survey
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
3
Justification Production within AFC
AFC consists of multiple stages forming a pipeline,
as shown in Figure 1
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
One of these stages is justi-
fication production
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In the upcoming subsections,
a brief overview of the general stages in the AFC
pipeline is provided, with a specific focus on the
justification production stage
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
1
Check-worthy Claims Detection Stage
This initial stage classifies the claims as check-
worthy or not
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
If they are check-worthy, then
they are selected from the corpus containing them
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Deeming if a claim is check-worthy or not is based
on the importance of the topic of the claim, if it is
verifiable, and if the claim poses potential harm in
case it is misleading (Guo et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
2
Retrieval and Selection of Most Relevant
Evidence
This stage retrieves data related to the claim from
trustworthy sources and selects the most relevant
information to make a decision, which is termed
the ‘evidence
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
’ In the subsequent stage, this evi-
dence is used to predict the veracity of the claim
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The determination of veracity depends on the
degree of alignment between the claim and the
evidence
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
For example, the veracity of the claim
‘The director of the film ‘Legend’ is English’ could
depend on the following evidence snippets gath-
ered from multiple trustworthy websites: ‘Brian
Helgeland is the director of the film ‘Legend”, and
‘Brian Helgeland only holds a U
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
3
Veracity Prediction of the Claim
This stage classifies claims according to a binary
scheme, true or false, or through fine-grained multi-
class classification including also other verdicts
such as “partially correct”, or “correct but mislead-
ing without extra context”
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Following the example
in the previous section, the claim ‘The director of
the film ‘Legend’ is English’ should be determined
as ‘False’ as it is not aligned with the evidence
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
4
Justification Production
This stage produces justifications to explain the ver-
dict of an AFC model regarding a claim’s veracity
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The process is known as justification production
(Guo et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In the context of the previously discussed claim,
‘The director of the film ‘Legend’ is English,’ an
example of a justification for the ‘False’ verdict,
grounded in the evidence, could be ‘Brian Helge-
land, the director of the film ‘Legend,’ is American
and not English
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
’ Hence, the inputs for a justifica-
tion production component are the claim and the
selected evidence
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The veracity verdict may also be
an input, depending on the pipeline architectures
of the AFC systems that are explained in Section
6
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
3 and are shown in our proposed classification of
pipelines (see Figure 2)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
We propose categorizing the work in justifica-
tion production not only based on these pipeline
architectures but also on additional dimensions (see
Figure 3)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
A key dimension is the explainability
of the justification production process
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The steps of
the process leading to the prediction of the claim’s
veracity and its justification can be self-explainable
or not
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In addition, the input type is an important di-
mension
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
It can be either multi-modal or text-
only
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Another dimension is the nature of the jus-
tification output
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
It may be natural language
text, or just highlighted parts of the input, like
bold/highlighted words in the claim and evidence,
or specific factual triples in the form Subject, Pred-
icate, Object (SPO) (see Figure 4 for illustrative
examples)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The relevant evidence rationals are selected nodes
in the graph, and the edges represent the relations
between these selected nodes
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
; and LLMs Retrieval Augmented Generation
(RAG) or Fine-tuning based approaches where
LLMs are used via prompting to verify the align-
ment between the claim and the evidence rationals
producing the veracity verdict and the justification
for the verdict
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

The quality of the justifica-
tions produced by these approaches is evaluated
based on the presence of certain desired properties,
which will be discussed in Section 4
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
4
Progression towards Justifications
Standardization
The aim of justification production is to create a
justification that aligns with specific, agreed-upon
criteria (Sokol and Flach, 2019), which we refer to
as a standard justification
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Achieving high-quality
justifications involves considering certain desired
properties known as ‘desiderata’ (Kulesza et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Researchers have collectively agreed upon
these desired properties (Sokol and Flach, 2019)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Producing justifications aligned with these desired
properties is crucial for standardizing justifications
in explainable AFC
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
These properties were defined with
further details by Kotonya and Toni (2020a), who
also added the desideratum of unbiased or impar-
tial justifications
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
In the context of fact-checking,
bias usually manifests as opinions masquerading
as evidence
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
To main-
tain local coherence, there should be no contradic-
tion between any two sentences in the justification
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
(2022) started the first attempt to
generate standard justifications by adding some de-
sired properties (i
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
faithfulness/coherence, and
data consistency) as additional learning signals
in the loss function of a transformer-based model
(Vaswani et al
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The data consistency evalu-
ates the similarity of justifications for similar input
instances
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
5
Datasets in AFC
It’s worth noting that this survey focuses on pro-
viding a new taxonomy, a comparative analysis
of justification production approaches, investigat-
ing pipeline architectures, addressing challenges
encountered, and proposing future directions in
AFC justification production
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Comprehensive ex-
aminations of datasets were covered thoroughly in
previous surveys (mentioned in Section 2)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
How-
ever, some information about datasets in AFC is
also provided in this section
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The dataset might contain the needed content for
all the stages of the fact-checking pipeline: claim
detection, evidence retrieval and selection, verac-
ity verdict production, and justification production
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
The following paragraphs will discuss the type
of content representing each stage with example
datasets provided in Table 1
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Textual claims are the most common input for
fact-checking because they are often produced after
the claim detection stage

text:
Many researchers have
created datasets by collecting real-world claims
from specialized websites like Politifact because
they are easily accessible
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Some researchers con-
centrate on obtaining claims from specialized ar-
eas like climate change, science, and public health
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Other sentence-level inputs, such as answers to
questions in forums, have also been investigated
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Some datasets are English, and some are multi-
lingual
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Metadata, including information such
as publication date, sources, and user profiles,
is a common type of evidence considered
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Al-
though metadata can provide complementary in-
sights when textual sources or structural knowl-
edge are lacking, it does not directly substantiate
the claim
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Text-based sources, such as news articles, aca-
demic publications, and Wikipedia entries, are fre-
quently employed as evidence for fact-checking
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Multi-modal claims and evidence have recently
been researched, and images and videos are consid-
ered more credible than text by most audiences
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Not all the datasets have a binary veracity ver-
dict scheme
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Fact-checkers often utilize multi-class
labels to classify different levels of truthfulness, in-
cluding categories such as ‘true,’ ‘mostly true,’ and
‘mixed
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
’
From the perspective of justification production,
AFC datasets can be classified into two categories:
those without justifications and those with justi-
fications
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Complementing datasets lacking justifi-
cations is important
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

Table 1 includes
datasets from both categories, with some being

6
Justification Production Taxonomy
Multiple dimensions or criteria for categorizing Ex-
plainable Automated Fact-Checking systems are
outlined in this section

We propose five dimen-
sions (illustrated by the first five levels/columns
of the Taxonomy tree in Figure 3)
paper_name: Automated Justification Production for Claim Veracity in Fact Checking A Survey on Architectures and Approaches 007

text:
Check-worthy claim detection across topics
for automated fact-checking
Amani S
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Despite a body of research tackling the task, previous
research has overlooked the challenging nature of identifying check-worthy claims
across different topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In this article, we assess and quantify the challenge of
detecting check-worthy claims for new, unseen topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
After highlighting the
problem, we propose the AraCWA model to mitigate the performance deterioration
when detecting check-worthy claims across topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
The AraCWA model enables
boosting the performance for new topics by incorporating two components for few-
shot learning and data augmentation
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Using a publicly available dataset of Arabic
tweets consisting of 14 different topics, we demonstrate that our proposed data
augmentation strategy achieves substantial improvements across topics overall,
where the extent of the improvement varies across topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Further, we analyse the
semantic similarities between topics, suggesting that the similarity metric could be
used as a proxy to determine the difﬁculty level of an unseen topic prior to
undertaking the task of labelling the underlying sentences
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
To alleviate
this challenge, support from automated fact-checking systems is becoming increasingly
necessary to help getting rid of misinformation (Zeng, Abumansour & Zubiaga, 2021)
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
One
of the key components of an automated fact-checking pipeline is the check-worthy claim
detection system, which given a collection of sentences as input, ranks them based on their
need to be fact-checked (Shaar et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
The ranked output can be used in different
ways, among others by providing fact-checkers with the most important claims to work on,
by feeding a claim veriﬁcation system with the top claims to be checked, or by informing
the general public that the top claims may be disputed and need veriﬁcation
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In recent years, there has been a body of research developing models for check-worthy
claim detection (Konstantinovskiy et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Many of these systems have been tested
against each other by competing on benchmark datasets released as part of the CheckThat
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

The recent tendency of these models has been to use

and tested on datasets that provide a mix of topics in both training and test data, in such a
way that topics encountered in the test set were already seen during training

The objective of our work is to assess the extent of the problem of detecting check-

, simulating the scenario where new topics emerge and for
which labelled data is lacking or limited

To undertake this challenge, we use an Arabic
language dataset from the CheckThat

shared tasks which comprises a collection of check-
worthy and not check-worthy claims distributed across 14 different topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
We ﬁrst
conduct experiments in zero-shot settings using a model called AraCW, which has not
seen any data associated with the target topic; the low performance of this model for some
of the topics demonstrates the importance of this hitherto unstudied problem in check-
worthy claim detection
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
We further propose AraCWA, an improved model that
incorporates two additional components to enable few-shot learning and data
augmentation
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
We test AraCWA with a range of few-shot and data augmentation settings
to evaluate its effectiveness, achieving improvements of up to 11% over AraCW
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
To the best of our knowledge, our work is the ﬁrst to tackle the check-worthy claim
detection task across topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In doing so, we make the following novel contributions:
 We establish a methodology for cross-topic claim detection in both zero-shot and few-
shot settings
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
 We assess the extent of the challenge of detecting claims for new, unseen topics through
zero-shot experiments
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
 We propose an improved approach, AraCWA, which leverages few-shot learning in
combination with data augmentation strategies to boost the performance on new topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
 We perform a set of ablative experiments to assess the contribution of the data
augmentation technique as well as the few-shot strategy
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
 We perform an analysis of the characteristics of the topics under study, ﬁnding that the
semantic similarity between topics can be a reasonable proxy to determine the difﬁculty
level of a topic
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In presenting the ﬁrst study in cross-topic claim detection, our work also posits the need
for more research in this challenging yet realistic scenario
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
RELATED WORK ON CHECK-WORTHY CLAIM DETECTION
While a large portion of the research in automated fact-checking has focused on claim
veriﬁcation (Pradeep et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, 2021; Schuster, Fisch & Barzilay, 2021; Zeng & Zubiaga, 2022),
Abumansour and Zubiaga (2023), PeerJ Comput
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text: work on check-worthy claim detection has been more limited
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
However, the claim
detection task constitutes an important ﬁrst step that identiﬁes the claims to be prioritised
and to be fed to the subsequent veriﬁcation component (Hassan et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
For
instance, fact-checkers are interested in prioritising claims that may have an impact in
society and hence are worthy of being checked
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Table 1 shows examples of two check-
worthy and not check-worthy claim for the same topic
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In what follows, we discuss existing
work in check-worthy claim detection with a particular focus on the Arabic language and
data augmentation
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Existing research in check-worthy claim detection has primarily focused on sentences in
the English language (Zeng, Abumansour & Zubiaga, 2021)
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
This is the case, for example,
of one of the ﬁrst fact-checking systems developed in the ﬁeld, ClaimBuster (Hassan et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
,
2017a, 2017b), which studied detection of claims from transcripts of US presidential
election debates through what they called “claim spotter”
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
(2019) investigated the use of context and discourse features to improve the claim
detection component; while this is a clever approach, it does not generalise to other types
of datasets such as tweets in our case, given the limited context available typically in social
media
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
(2021) developed a model, namely CNC, that
leverages InferSent embeddings (Conneau et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, 2017) along with part-of-speech tags and
named entities to identify claims
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In addition to the above studies, there have been numerous competitive efforts to
develop claim detection systems thanks to the CheckThat
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
These shared tasks have provided a benchmark for
researchers to study the task in a competitive manner, including publicly available datasets
in multiple languages
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In our study, we use the datasets available from these shared tasks in
the Arabic language
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Check-worthy claim detection in the Arabic language
Check-worthy claim detection for the Arabic language was ﬁrst tackled by ClaimRank
(Jaradat et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, 2018) when training the neural network model with originally English
sentences from political debates translated into Arabic
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, Gradient boosting and k-nearest neighbors, were inspected for the task in Arabic of
check-worthiness at CheckThat
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
The paradigm shift in the ﬁeld of NLP occurred with the emergence of Transformers
(Wolf et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, 2020), and bidirectional encoder representations (BERT), which became the
state-of-art model for several tasks, including a range of text classiﬁcation tasks (Devlin
et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
This is also reﬂected in the works on check-worthy claim detection as most
participants of the recent CheckThat Labs ﬁne-tuned BERT models for Arabic such as
mBERT, AraBERT, and BERT-Base-Arabic (Hasanain et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
However, existing models focus on evaluating their performance on test sets whose
topics overlap with those seen in the training data, without focusing on the challenges that
different topics may pose to the task
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
As part of this study, we focus on the novel challenge
of investigating how claim detection models perform differently for different topics
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Data augmentation for claim detection
Data augmentation techniques enable to leverage the (generally limited) existing data to
generate new synthetic data through alterations
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
By carefully altering the original data
samples, one can augment the available training data with the newly generated samples;
however, data augmentation techniques may also produce noisy samples which lead to
performance deterioration, and therefore creation of useful samples is crucial
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Data
augmentation strategies have also been successfully used to alleviate different problems,
e
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text: , to settle an imbalanced dataset and to reduce model biases (Feng et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text: Data augmentation techniques have been barely used in claim detection
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
For example,
Williams, Rodrigues & Tran (2021) used a contextual word embedding augmentation
strategy in their participation in the 2021 CheckThat
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
These data augmentation
techniques have however not been studied in the context of cross-topic claim detection
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
In
our study, we propose the AraCWA model which provides the ﬂexibility of being used
with different data augmentation strategies
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
More speciﬁcally, we test three data
augmentation strategies to evaluate their effectiveness in the cross-topic claim detection
task, namely back-translation, contextual word embedding augmentation and text-
generating augmentation
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Rumour detection
Related areas of research have focused on rumour detection (Derczynski et al
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
, 2021), which have aspects in common with claim
detection as they also consist in ﬁnding sentences that need linking to evidence
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Rumour
detection is generally performed over breaking news events, which are of different nature
compared to the claim detection task which typically focuses on other kinds of claims
Abumansour and Zubiaga (2023), PeerJ Comput
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Despite the commonalities between the two tasks, we are not
aware of work detecting rumours across topics, not least in the Arabic language
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
METHODOLOGY
Problem formulation and evaluation
The check-worthy claim detection task consists in determining which of the sentences, out
of a collection, constitute claims that should be fact-checked
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
Given a collection of
sentences S ¼ fs1; s2; …; sng, the model needs to classify each of them into one of
L ¼ fCW; NCWg, where CW = check-worthy and NCW = not check-worthy
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
The
collection of sentences in S belongs to a number of topics T ¼ ft1; t2; …; tmg, where each
sentence sj only belongs to one topic ti
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
While the claim detection task has sometimes been formulated as a classiﬁcation
problem, here and in line with the CheckThat
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
shared tasks, we formulate it as a ranking
task
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
As a ranking problem, the model needs to produce an ordered list of sentences in S,
where the sentences in the top of the list are predicted as the most likely to be check-worthy
claims
paper_name: Check-worthy claim detection across topics 003

text:
The task is in turn evaluated through the mean average precision (MAP) metric,
which is deﬁned as follows:

After

The
datasets contain tweets which are labelled as check-worthy or not check-worthy

The
tweets are also categorised into topics, which allows us to set up the cross-topic
experiments

Abstract
The large and ever-increasing amount of data available on
the Internet coupled with the laborious task of manual claim
and fact verification has sparked the interest in the develop-
ment of automated claim verification systems
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
1 Several deep
learning and transformer-based models have been proposed
for this task over the years
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
With the introduction of Large
Language Models (LLMs) and their superior performance in
several NLP tasks, we have seen a surge of LLM-based ap-
proaches to claim verification along with the use of novel
methods such as Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
In
this survey, we present a comprehensive account of recent
claim verification frameworks using LLMs
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We describe the
different components of the claim verification pipeline used
in these frameworks in detail including common approaches
to retrieval, prompting, and fine-tuning
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Finally, we describe
publicly available English datasets created for this task
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Introduction
False information is widely present on social media and on
the Web motivating the development of automated fact ver-
ification systems (Guo, Schlichtkrull, and Vlachos 2022)
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
The introduction of LLMs has provided malicious actors
with sophisticated ways of creating and disseminating false
information
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Recent election cycles saw a large number of
claims spread across both social media and news platforms
alike (Dmonte et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Similarly, during the COVID-
19 pandemic, many claims were spread across social media
platforms
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Many of these claims were factually inaccurate
which led to the spread of misinformation (Zhou et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Fact-checking potentially false claims is an essential mod-
eration task to reduce the spread of misinformation
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Several
organizations such as FactCheck, PolitiFact, NewsGuard,
and Full Fact perform manual fact-checking to verify claims
in different domains
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
However, this has been regarded as a
laborious task that requires domain expertise (Adair et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2017; Hanselowski 2020) and it has become less and less
feasible due to the sheer volume of misinformation that can
be generated by humans and AI models
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Automated fact-
checking has become an increasingly popular approach to

text:
2 There are sev-
eral steps involved in the fact-verification pipelines, but the
three main components are claim detection, evidence re-
trieval, and veracity prediction
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Models used in these steps
have followed the general methodological developments of
the field and we have thus observed an increase in the use
of LLMs for claim verification (Zhang and Gao 2023; Wang
and Shu 2023; Quelle and Bovet 2024)
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

Figure 1 shows an

based claim verification systems
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

Compared to traditional
systems which use NLP-based models, fact verification are
less prone to error propagation, and provide explainable/in-

applied to other modalities of data (e

In this survey, we

paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Despite this, LLMs are pre-trained on very large collec-
tions of texts and are prone to hallucinations, often gener-
ating texts containing incorrect information
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Furthermore,
such models can be used to generate misinformation at scale
(Chen and Shu 2023; Zhou et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2024)
and therefore they can be exploited by malicious actors to
generate and spread wrong and factually incorrect informa-
tion at an unprecedented rate (Pan et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Further-
more, using these pre-trained models for fact-verification
may generate incorrect veracity labels, as the models may
also rely on obsolete information to assess the veracity of a
claim
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2023), which aid
the models in their decision-making abilities are used in this
task for the model to get access to the most recent informa-
tion during the fact-verification task
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2023) and applica-
tions to specialized domains such as scientific texts (Vladika
and Matthes 2023)
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
However, all past related surveys, lack
consideration for LLM-based approaches or focus on spe-
cific sub-tasks of the pipeline (Panchendrarajan and Zubi-
aga 2024)
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
In this paper, we fill this important gap by sur-
veying LLM-based frameworks proposed in recent years
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
To
the best of our knowledge, this is the first survey to explore
claim verification with LLMs
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We expect it to be a valuable
resource to researchers in the field opening exciting new av-
enues for future research
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Search Criteria
We search various well-known repositories3 of scientific ar-
ticles to collect the papers that serve as primary sources
3ACM: portal
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We apply queries including LLMs, claim
verification, fact verification and related keywords on these
repositories
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We focus primarily on the ACL Anthology, the
ACM Digital Library, and IEEE Xplore, and proceedings
of related conferences such as AAAI and IJCAI
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We further
search on Scopus, Springer Link, and Science@Direct, and
ArXiv
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We collected and reviewed over 100 papers based on
our search terms and filter papers based on the following
search criteria: (i) since the scope of our review is on text-
based LLM claim verification systems, we omit papers re-
lated to multimodal approaches (e
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
, text-to-images), our
papers from other modalities in claim verification (e
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(ii) since our review focuses on
LLM-based systems for claim veracity, we omit papers re-
lated to claim identification, claim detection, and/or detect-
ing LLM-generated content
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Based on our filtering criteria,
we collected a total of 49 papers related to LLM-based ap-
proaches for veracity labeling
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
The papers on topic LLM-
based veracity labeling have been published primarily in the
ACL Anthology and ACM Digital Library, but also in other
repositories such as the IEEE Xplore
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
We release this curated
list to be publicly available
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Many pro-
posed systems make use of only some of these modules
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
The
following subsections describe each of these modules in de-
tail
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Finally, as described in Panchendrarajan and Zubiaga
(2024), performance evaluation in these sub-tasks is usually
carried out using well-established automatic evaluation met-
rics used in text classification such as Precision, Recall, and
F-score
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Claim Detection
Input texts may contain one or more
statements but not all statements are claims
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Given the input
text, a claim detection module is designed to identify all the

text:
Instead of traditional automatic claim verification, this
pipeline creates a prompt from the retrieved evidence and the input claim as input to the LLM to generate a label, sentence
evidence, and/or explanation of its response
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
For example, the statement
’I loved the movie Oppenheimer
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
’ is an opinion, whereas the
statement ’The COVID-19 pandemic started in Texas
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Check-worthy Claim Identification
Not all the identified
claims are check-worthy
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
In the check-worthy claim identi-
fication sub-task, given an input claim, the task is to identify
the claims that include real-world assertions and that may
need to be verified (Hassan, Li, and Tremayne 2015; Nakov
et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
This is a subjective task and it relies on the
factors like popularity of the claim, public interest in deter-
mining the veracity of the claim, etc
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
For example, the claim
’The President met the State Governor to discuss the infras-
tructure deal
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
’ is less check-worthy than the claim ’Drinking
salt water cures COVID
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Claim Matching
The identified check-worthy claims can
be matched to the previously fact-checked claims
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Given
an input claim and a database of previously fact-checked
claims, claim matching is used to determine if the input
claim is previously fact-checked and exists in the database
(Shaar et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
This can help avoid
the next steps in the pipeline and a veracity label can be pre-
dicted directly
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Document/Evidence Retrieval
If the input claim does not
exist in the database of fact-checked claims, the claim needs
to be verified
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
In the document or evidence retrieval sub-
task, all the relevant documents related to the input claim
are extracted, either from an external database or through
an internet search/information retrieval(Chen et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
A
threshold of how many documents are to be retrieved can be
predetermined
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Rationale/Sentence Selection
All the information in the
retrieved documents is not relevant to the claim
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Hence in
the rationale selection task, only the information or evidence
most relevant to the claim are selected to be used to predict
the veracity label
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Veracity Label Prediction
Once the rationale is selected,
the claim along with the rationale and additional features
if any, are given as input to a machine learning model
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
The
task of the classifier is to predict a veracity label from among
the following three labels; ’SUPPORTED’, ’REFUTED’, or
’NOT ENOUGH EVIDENCE’
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
In some datasets, the labels
can also be ’TRUE’, or ’FALSE’, depending on the specific
task
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Explanation/Justification
Generation
Recent
works
have focused on generating explanations for the veracity
labels prediction
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
This specific task is focused on generating
natural language justifications or explanations for the
prediction considering the claim and evidence to generate
these explanations
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
LLM Approaches
In this section, we describe the recent advancements that en-
able LLMs to be robust in fact verification scenarios
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Fig-
ure 3 shows an example pipeline that encapsulates multi-
ple component modules (i
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
, Evidence Retrieval, Prompt
Creation, Transfer Learning, and LLM Generation) for ver-
ifying claims
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Different from traditional fact verification
pipelines that select evidence set for verifying claims, LLM-
based claim verification conditions generated text based on
the concatenated input claim and retrieved evidence
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
The
ability of large, pre-trained LMs augmented with retrieval
enables them to perform well on knowledge-intensive tasks
such as text generation
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Evidence Retrieval Strategies
RAG models, which have been developed to combat the is-
sue of hallucination in LLMs in knowledge-intensive tasks,
have shown success in the scope of fact verification (Lewis
et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(2020) developed a
framework that incorporates a retriever to retrieve evidence
from an external database such as Wikipedia for condition-
ally generating veracity labels in fact verification
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(2023) demonstrate that RAGs perform well
on the FEVER shared task (Thorne et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2018b)) in few-
shot settings, showing approximately 5% improvement over
large-scale LLMs such as Gopher (Rae et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
2021) with sig-
nificantly fewer parameters (i
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Other works consider the optimization of either document
ranking (Glass et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Tan 2024) as a crucial step for improving evidence retrieval
for veracity labeling
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(2023) use
an autoregressive re-ranker to get the most relevant passages
from the retriever
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
These are then passed to the generation
model to generate the veracity label
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Despite this, RAG models can often fail when encoun-
tering long or complex input claims, causing the model to
incorrectly generate veracity labels or evidence sentences
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Recent works have addressed this issue by segmenting long
claims into smaller sub-claims and performing multiple
rounds of retrieval (Khattab, Potts, and Zaharia 2021; Shao
et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Authors in Khattab, Potts, and Zaharia (2021)
present a pipeline for multi-hop claim verification that uses
an iterative retriever and neural methods for effective doc-
ument retrieval and re-ranking
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(2023) show
that using a re-ranker to distill knowledge to a retriever
helps close the semantic gaps between a query and docu-
ment passage when verifying claims using iterative RAGs
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Other works address the issue of complex claims by using
fine-grained retrieval techniques based on claim decompo-
sition (Chen et al
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
(2023a) generate sub-questions based on
a claim, which a document retriever uses to retrieve rele-
vant documents
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
A fine-grained retriever retrieves top-k text
spans as evidence based on a k-word window and BM25
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
Authors in Zhang and Gao (2023) decompose a claim into
sub-claims and generate questions to verify the sub-claims
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

text:
External knowledge sources are used to retrieve relevant in-
formation to verify the sub-claims and generate a final verac-
ity label
paper_name: Claim Verification in the Age of Large Language Models A Survey

NEWSCLAIMS: A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News
with Attribute Knowledge
Revanth Gangi Reddy1, Sai Chetan Chinthakindi1, Zhenhailong Wang1, Yi R
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Fung1,
Kathryn Conger2, Ahmed Elsayed2, Martha Palmer2, Preslav Nakov3,
Eduard Hovy4,5, Kevin Small6, Heng Ji1

paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
edu
Abstract
Claim detection and veriﬁcation are crucial
for news understanding and have emerged
as promising technologies for mitigating mis-
information and disinformation in the news
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
However, most existing work has focused on
claim sentence analysis while overlooking ad-
ditional crucial attributes (e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, the claimer and
the main object associated with the claim)
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In
this work, we present NEWSCLAIMS, a new
benchmark for attribute-aware claim detection
in the news domain
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We extend the claim de-
tection problem to include extraction of ad-
ditional attributes related to each claim and
release 889 claims annotated over 143 news
articles
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
NEWSCLAIMS1 aims to benchmark
claim detection systems in emerging scenar-
ios, comprising unseen topics with little or no
training data
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
To this end, we see that zero-
shot and prompt-based baselines show promis-
ing performance on this benchmark, while still
considerably behind human performance
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
1
Introduction
The internet era has ushered in an explosion of
online content creation, resulting in increased con-
cerns regarding misinformation in news, online
debates, and social media
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
A key element of iden-
tifying misinformation is detecting the claims and
the arguments that have been presented
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In this
regard, news articles are particularly interesting as
they contain claims in various formats: from ar-
guments by journalists to reported statements by
prominent public ﬁgures
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Check-worthiness estimation aims to decide if a
piece of text is worth fact-checking, i
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, whether it
contains an important veriﬁable factual claim (Has-
san et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2021) largely ignore
relevant attributes of the claim (e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, the claimer
and the primary object associated with the claim)
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
1The code and data have been made publicly available
here: https://github
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
com/blender-nlp/NewsClaims
Figure 1: A news article containing a claim regarding
the origin of COVID-19 with the claim sentence in ital-
ics, the claim span in red, and the claimer in blue
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Also
shown are the claimer stance and the claim object
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Moreover, current claim detection tasks mainly
identify claims in debates (Gencheva et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2019a), and social me-
dia (Nakov et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
News articles, on the other hand, have more com-
plex arguments, requiring a deeper understanding
of what each claim is about and identifying where
it comes from
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Thus, here we introduce the notion
of claim object, which we deﬁne as an entity that
identiﬁes what is being claimed with respect to the
topic of the claim
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Figure 1 shows a claim about
the origin of COVID-19, suggesting that the virus
came from space, which is the claim object
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We fur-
ther identify the claimer, which could be useful for
fact-checking organizations to examine how cur-
rent claims compare to previous ones by the same
person/organization
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In this regard, we extend the
claim detection task to ask for the extraction of
more attributes related to the claim
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Speciﬁcally,
given a news article, we aim to extract all claims
pertaining to a set of topics along with the corre-
sponding claim span, the claimer, the claimer’s
stance, and the claim object for each claim
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
The
claim attributes enable comparing claims at a more
ﬁne-grained level: claims with the same topic,
object and stance can be considered equivalent
arXiv:2112
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
whereas those with similar claim objects but op-
posing stance could be contradicting
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We note that
while identifying the claim span and stance have
been explored independently in prior work (Levy
et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2021a), we bring them
into the purview of a uniﬁed claim detection task
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
To promote research in this direction, we release
NEWSCLAIMS, a new evaluation benchmark for
claim detection
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We consider this in an evaluation
setting since claims about new topics can emerge
rapidly2, requiring systems that are effective un-
der zero/few-shot settings
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
NEWSCLAIMS aims to
study how existing NLP techniques can be lever-
aged to tackle claim detection in emerging scenar-
ios and regarding previously unseen topics
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We
explore multiple zero/few-shot strategies for our
subtasks including topic classiﬁcation, stance detec-
tion, and claim object detection
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
This is in line with
recent progress in using pre-trained language mod-
els in zero/few-shot settings (Brown et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Such approaches can be adapted
to new use cases and problems as they arise without
the need for large additional training data
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In our benchmark, all news articles are related
to the COVID-19 pandemic, motivated by multiple
considerations
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
First, COVID-19 has gained exten-
sive media coverage, with the World Health Organi-
zation coining the term infodemic3 to refer to disin-
formation related to COVID-19 (Naeem and Bhatti,
2020) and suggesting that “fake news spreads faster
and more easily than this virus”
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Second, this is
an emerging scenario with limited previous data
related to the virus, making it a suitable candidate
for evaluating claim detection in a low-resource
setting
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
NEWSCLAIMS covers claims about four
COVID-19 topics, namely the origin of the virus,
possible cure for the virus, the transmission of the
virus, and protecting against the virus
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
2harmful-content-blog-post
3COVID-19 Infodemic
2
Related Work
Automatic fact-checking has a number of sub-tasks
such as detecting check-worthy claims (Jaradat
et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2019), comparing them
against previously-fact checked claims (Shaar et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2021), retrieving evidence rele-
vant to a claim (Karadzhov et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2019) and ﬁnally inferring the veracity of
the claim (Karadzhov et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Our work here is
positioned in the space of identifying check-worthy
claims, also known as check-worthiness estimation
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In this work, we show that identifying the topic
of the claim is beneﬁcial, by leveraging it towards
stance detection (Section 5
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
3) and claim object de-
tection (Section 5
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Argumentation mining (Palau and Moens, 2009;
Stab and Gurevych, 2014; Stab et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2018) in-
cludes context-dependent claim detection (Levy
et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2014, 2017), which entails detecting claims
speciﬁcally relevant to a predeﬁned topic
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
However,
claims in the context of argumentation are neither
necessarily factual nor veriﬁable
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Moreover, prior
work on both check-worthiness estimation and ar-
gumentation mining did not deal with identifying
additional claim attributes, such as the claimer, or
the source of the claim, and the claim object
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
The claimer detection subtask is related to attri-
bution in the news
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Current attribution methods
are mainly sentence-level (Pareti, 2016a) or only
involve direct quotations (Elson and McKeown,
2010)
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In contrast, we require cross-sentence rea-
soning for identifying the claimer as it may not be
present in the claim sentence (see Figure 1)
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
There has been recent work addressing claims re-
lated to COVID-19
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
(2021) proposed
a new FEVER-like (Thorne et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2018) dataset,
where given a claim, the task is to identify relevant
evidence and to verify whether it refutes or supports
the claim; however, this does not tackle identifying
the claims or the claimer
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
There has also been work
on identifying the check-worthiness of tweets re-
lated to COVID-19 (Alam et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
,
2021); however, unlike news articles, tweets do not
require attribution for claimer identiﬁcation
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
3
Proposed Claim Detection Task
Our task is to identify claims related to a set of
topics in a news article along with corresponding
attributes such as the claimer, the claim object, and
the claim span and stance, as shown in Figure 2
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text: Figure 2: An example demonstrating our proposed claim detection task, and its subtasks
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
The following attributes
are to be extracted for each claim: the claimer, claimer’s stance, claim object, and claim span
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Claim Sentence Detection: Given a news article,
the ﬁrst subtask is to extract claim sentences rele-
vant to a set of pre-deﬁned topics
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
This involves
ﬁrst identifying sentences that contain factually
veriﬁable claims, similar to prior work on check-
worthiness estimation, and then selecting those that
are related to the target topics
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
To address misin-
formation in an emerging real-world setting, we
consider the following topics related to COVID-19:
Origin of the virus: claims related to the origin
of the virus (i
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, location of ﬁrst detection, zoono-
sis, ‘lab leak’ theories); Transmission of the virus:
claims related to who/what can transmit the virus or
conditions favorable for viral transmission; Cure
for the virus: claims related to curing the virus,
(e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, via medical intervention after infection); and
Protection from the virus: claims related to pre-
cautions against viral infection
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Claimer Detection: Claims within a news article
can come from various types of sources such as
an entity (e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, person, organization) or published
artifact (e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In such
cases, the claimer identity can usually be extracted
from the news article itself
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
However, if the claim
is asserted by the article author or if no attribu-
tion is speciﬁed or inferrable, then the article au-
thor, i
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
the journalist, is considered to be the
claimer
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
The claimer detection subtask involves
identifying whether the claim is made by a journal-
ist or whether it is reported in the news article, in
which case the source is also extracted
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Moreover,
sources of such reported claims need not be within
the claim sentence
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
In our datatset NEWSCLAIMS,
the claimer span was extracted from outside of the
claim sentence for about 47% of the claims
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Thus,
the claimer detection subtask in our benchmark
requires considerable document-level reasoning,
thus making it harder than existing attribution tasks
(Pareti, 2016b; Newell et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2018), which require
only sentence-level reasoning
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Claim Object Detection: The claim object relates
to what is being claimed in the claim sentence with
respect to the topic
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
For example, in a claim regard-
ing the virus origin, the claim object could be the
species of origin in zoonosis claims, or who created
the virus in bioengineering claims
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Table 1 shows
examples of claim objects from each topic
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We see
that the claim object is usually an extractive span
within the claim sentence
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Identifying the claim
object helps to better understand the claims and po-
tentially identify claim–claim relations, since two
claims with the same object are likely to be similar
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Topic
Claim Sentence
Origin
The genetic data is pointing to this virus
coming from a bat reservoir, he said
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Transmission
The virus lingers in the air indoors, in-
fecting those nearby
Cure
Vitamin C is an effective treatment for
COVID-19
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Protection
Taking a hot bath prevents you from get-
ting COVID-19
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Table 1: Examples showing the claim object in bold for
claims corresponding to NEWSCLAIMS topics
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Stance Detection:
This subtask involves out-
putting whether the claimer is asserting (afﬁrm)
or refuting (refute) a claim within the given claim
sentence
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
We note that stance detection in NEWS-
CLAIMS differs from the task formulation used in
other stance detection datasets (Stab et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, 2019; Allaway and McKeown,
2020) as it involves identifying the claimer’s stance
within a claim sentence – whereas prior stance de-
tection tasks, as described in a recent survey by
Hardalov et al
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
(2021b), involve identifying the
stance for target–context pairs
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
For example, given
pairs such as claim–evidence or headline–article, it
involves identifying whether the evidence/article at
hand supports or refutes a given claim/headline
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Claim Span Detection: Given a claim sentence,
this subtask aims to identify the exact claim bound-
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
(a) claim counts per news article
(b) claims by journalists vs
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
reported
ones, along with claimer coverage for
reported claims
(c) coverage of claimer within a win-
dow size based on number of sentences
around the claim sentence
Figure 3: NEWSCLAIMS benchmark statistics: (a) number of claims per news article, (b) claims by journalists vs
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text: reported claims, and (c) claimer coverage by window size within the news article for reported claims
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
aries within the sentence, including the actual claim
content, usually without any cue words (e
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
, as-
serted, suggested) and frequently a contiguous sub-
span of the claim sentence
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

text:
Identifying the precise
claim conveyed within the sentence can be useful
for downstream tasks such as clustering claims and
identifying similar or opposing claims
paper_name: NEWSCLAIMS A New Benchmark for Claim Detection from News 004

By leveraging insights from philosophy 
and linguistics, PCD is a more systematic and transparent version of claim detection 
than previous approaches

This paper presents the theoretical background for PCD 
and discusses its advantages over alternative approaches to claim detection

Mod-
els trained on the dataset show stable performance for text from previously unseen 
domains such as different topical domains or writing styles

PCD is a basic task that

tional tools
