text:
PCD is in line with previous approaches to claim 
detection that aim at the support of fact-checkers by identifying claims that poten-
tially carry misinformation [2]
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Sentences that can be true or false are known in philosophy and mathematical 
logic as sentences with propositional content
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
As propositional sentences are almost 
exclusively declarative sentences, PCD mostly leverages grammatical information 
(e
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: , word order, punctuation, or tense) for classification
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Grammatical or syntacti-
cal information has often been neglected in computational methods for social sci-
entific research [3]
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
However, it has useful properties that are beneficial to methods 
like PCD
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
A core assumption of this paper is that the grammatical information that 
is leveraged by PCD remains relatively stable across domains and accordingly PCD-
models can be used in various contexts and purposes
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The guiding questions of this 
paper are if PCD can reach competitive results to other approaches to claim detec-
tion and if these results remain stable across texts of different topical domain, time 
periods or writing styles
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: As it will be shown, both questions have a positive answer
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
This paper makes multiple contributions: (1) a new task design for the detection 
of claims is introduced, (2) a dataset for PCD is described and made publicly avail-
able, (3) an array of models is tested on the dataset with a special focus on domain 
adaptation
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

Background
Automated fact‑checking
Guo et al
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[4] provide a survey of different sub-tasks for automated fact-checking 
and find research on claim detection, retrieving previously checked claims, claim 
verification, and generating a textual justification for a verdict
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Especially claim ver-
ification has received much attention (see e
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
, [5, 6] for a survey on the related task 
of stance detection)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
For this task, a model is supposed to derive an evidence-based 
verdict (true, false, etc
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
However, there has been critique on this 
task
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[7] argue that many approaches are developed and tested in 
an unrealistic setup and cannot refute real-world misinformation
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[8] find that human fact-checkers have little trust in a fully-automated pipeline and 
rather find interest in tools like claim detection that only aim at partial automation
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Models for claim detection are trained to identify claims that potentially carry 
misinformation
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The aim is to decrease the workload of human fact-checkers by pro-
viding a pre-selection of relevant claims that can then be verified
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
A claim is usually 
understood as one isolated sentence and claim detection is most often designed as a 
binary sentence classification task
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[2] use 
a taxonomy with three classes, Konstantinovskiy et al
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[9] have seven classes, and

text:
However, often even these approaches 
are tackled in a binary format, too [11]
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The most notable approach to claim detection is the Claimbuster-project2 that 
contributed a widely used dataset [2] and built models that achieved strong results 
on it [11]
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
In their, and most other formulations, claim detection is the task of clas-
sifying checkworthy claims (Table  1)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Checkworthiness is defined differently by 
different authors
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[2] checkworthy claims are understood 
as “factual claims that the general public will be interested in learning about their 
veracity”
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[20] asked the annotators to label a sentence as checkworthy 
if the they could affirm the following question:” Do you think that a professional 
fact-checker should verify the claim in the tweet
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
” A third approach was taken by, 
for example, Shaar et al
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[14], who matched sentences from US-presidential debates 
with factchecking articles by PolitiFact and labeled them as checkworthy if there 
was a corresponding article
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: This and similar definitions have also attracted criticism
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Allein and Moens [21] 
note that checkworthiness depends on the prior knowledge of a person, which makes 
the concept inherently subjective
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
They conclude that checkworthiness is an inapt 
concept to guide claim detection
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
One can add that the definition’s reliance on “the 
general public” is already idealized as the public is often fragmented and different 
social groups differ in their values or political ideologies
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Both aspects are relevant 
to determine if a claim should be fact-checked
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Arguing that checkworthiness is an editiorial decision that is best left to human 
fact-checkers, Konstantinowskiy et al
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[9] propose an alternative formulation of the 
claim detection task

statistics, legal affairs, or causal relationships and opposed to sentences about per-
sonal experiences like “I woke up this morning at 7 a
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[18] and 
Wilms [22] created a German language dataset for the same task
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
However, in their 
taxonomy, a positive example is a claim to truth or a sentence that provides exter-
nal evidence (links, quotes, etc
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: ) rather than internal evidence (personal experience)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[19] define a claim as stating or asserting that something is the case, 
with or without providing evidence or proof
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
They use a similar scheme as the previ-
ous authors but they also consider personal experiences and humor/sarcasm as posi-
tive instances
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: All three approaches reach strong results (see Table 1)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Computational linguistics
A line of research in computational linguistics that shares many similarities to 
PCD is Dialogue Act classification (DA)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
One famous corpus is the Switchboard 
Dialogue Act corpus (SwDA) that was originally introduced by Jurafsky et al
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
[23] 
and provides a fine-grained labeling scheme of 43 classes that involve, for exam-
ple, opinion-statements, non-opinion-statements, or different types of questions
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The 
task is approached as text classification but also as sequence labeling and there have 
been attempts [24] that reach scores of 82
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
DA and PCD follow the same theoretical tradition that is based on the works 
of Austin and Searle
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Argumentation Mining (AM) is closely related to claim detection as it aims at 
identifying the components of an argument and their relation
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Similar to claim detec-
tion, extracting claims lies at the heart of AM
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
However, their definitions are not the 
same
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: In AM a claim is understood as a conclusion rather than a premise [e
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
, [26]], 
while for claim detection both can be claims
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Moreover, often sentences or textual 
spans that are understood as claims in AM would rather qualify as opinions from the 
perspective of claim detection
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The structure of propositional claim detection
Propositional content
PCD is about detecting sentences with propositional content
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
This term is used in 
philosophy and mathematical logic and denotes sentences that have a truth value, 
i
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The concept of truth value is different from the 
concept of truth
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Sentences that have a truth value are possibly but not necessarily 
true
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: Take the sentences “All cows are ruminants
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
” Even without knowing the word “ruminant”, one can acknowl-
edge that the first sentence can be true or false but the second cannot
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Accordingly, 
we do not need to know if a sentence is true, in order to know that it has a truth 
value
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Note that sentences with truth values are a broader class than factual sen-
tences and the two sentence types should not be confused (see “Discussion” section)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
1731
1 3
Journal of Computational Social Science (2024) 7:1727–1752	
Sentences that carry a truth value meet two minimal conditions: (1) the sentence 
must have a condition of satisfaction and (2) this condition must have a word-to-
world direction of fit
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
For a sentence to have a condition of satisfaction is to have a 
relation to the world that can be fulfilled
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
3 For example, saying that Japan has a total 
population of (roughly) 125 million relates to the world and it is satisfied because 
Japan does have about 125 million citizens (a)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
Saying that Japan has the dirtiest 
streets worldwide also relates to the world but it is not satisfied because Japan’s 
streets are extremely clean (b)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
And if the Japanese digital minister pledges that the 
government will offer more digital services, his sentence relates to the world and it 
is satisfied if the government actually manages to provide more digital services (c)
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text: However, other sentence types like apologies or congratulations cannot be satisfied
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
If the digital minister of Japan apologizes for not providing more digital services, his 
utterance cannot be satisfied
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
The purpose of his statement is neither referring to a 
desired future state of affairs nor is it a statement that can be true or false
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

text:
It is meant 
as an apology, which is something different
paper_name: Propositional claim detection a task and dataset 005

Abstract 
Objective: To automate scientific claim verification using PubMed abstracts
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text: We also created an ensemble of three state-of-the-art deep learning models to classify rationale of support, refute, and neutral
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We then constructed CoVERt, a new COVID VERification dataset comprising 15 PICO-encoded drug claims accompanied by 96 manually 
selected and labeled clinical trial abstracts that either support or refute each claim
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We used CoVERt and SciFact (a public scientific claim verifi­
cation dataset) to assess CliVER’s performance in predicting labels
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Finally, we compared CliVER to clinicians in the verification of 19 claims 
from 6 disease domains, using 189 648 PubMed abstracts extracted from January 2010 to October 2021
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text: Results: In the evaluation of label prediction accuracy on CoVERt, CliVER achieved a notable F1 score of 0
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
92, highlighting the efficacy of the 
retrieval-augmented models
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The ensemble model outperforms each individual state-of-the-art model by an absolute increase from 3% to 11% 
in the F1 score
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text: Moreover, when compared with four clinicians, CliVER achieved a precision of 79
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Conclusion: CliVER demonstrates its early potential to automate scientific claim verification using retrieval-augmented strategies to harness 
the wealth of clinical trial abstracts in PubMed
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text: Future studies are warranted to further test its clinical utility
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text: Key words: natural language processing; clinical trial; evidence retrieval; deep learning; evidence appraisal
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Introduction
In an era of exponential growth of clinical publication, it has 
been increasingly challenging to identify the most relevant 
articles and extract relevant information for a given scientific 
claim
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
1 For example, LitCovid, the primary bibliographic 
database to track up-to-date published research on COVID- 
19 and SARS-CoV-2 on PubMed, has grown to more than 
300 000 relevant articles in about 2 years and continues pro­
liferating with 10 000 new articles added each month
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
As new evidence emerges, scientific understand­
ings of many topics constantly evolve
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
found that nearly one-third of highly cited clinical stud­
ies are eventually refuted as subsequent studies reach 
contradictions or find the original claim overstated
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
It is promising to mitigate these problems by implementing 
an automated framework that efficiently retrieves high- 
quality evidence for scientific claim verification
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Amongst the 
plethora of clinical publications, randomized controlled trials 
(RCTs) stand as the most reliable sources of medical evidence 
next to meta-analysis or systematic reviews, and are essential 
for both clinical question answering6,7 and evidence-based 
medicine practice
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
8,9 Therefore, here, we focus on retrieving 
RCT studies for biomedical scientific claim verification
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Verifying scientific claims falls under the larger domain of 
fact verification
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
10,11 In the biomedical domain, Wadden 
et al
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
introduced the concept of scientific claim verification 
with a three-step approach that includes “Document

modules
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
12 To facilitate the development and validation of 
these algorithms, the authors curated the SciFact12 dataset
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Notably, the first “Document Retrieval” module used text 
similarity to retrieve the most relevant abstracts without lev­
eraging language embeddings
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
13 applied a simi­
lar three-step framework but exploited the power of the T5 
language model
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
14 However, these studies may suffer from 
error propagation
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
15 proposed a 
paragraph-level, multi-task learning model and used BioSent­
Vec to compute the cosine similarities between claim and 
abstract embeddings
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
16 leveraged the Long­
former model17 to cross-encode the claim, title, and abstract 
together as input with minimal truncations
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
These studies 
achieved impressive performance in scientific claim verifica­
tion against a relatively small (<10 000) document reposi­
tory
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
They were not evaluated in real scientific use cases 
where hundreds of thousands of documents are queried 
against
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
To the best of our knowledge, none of these studies 
focus on RCTs, which are substantially different from obser­
vational epidemiological studies (eg, cohort, case-control, 
and cross-sectional studies) and other topics within biomedi­
cine (eg, laboratory-based research)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Identifying the best evidence requires massive RCTs to ena­
ble rigorous and reliable study appraisal—for example, rele­
vance, allocation concealment, intention to treat analysis, 
and relevant outcomes
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
18,19 Retrieval-augmented methodolo­
gies harness the capabilities of multiple information retrieval 
techniques (such as document vectorization,20,21 semantic 
similarity-based retrievers,22,23 and similarity ranking24,25
mechanisms)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The adoption of these techniques facilitates an 
efficient and accurate search through vast datasets of RCT 
studies, improving the identification of the most relevant 
evidence
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Existing claim verification datasets have a specific focus, 
making them suitable for applications in certain clinical 
domains
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For instance, FEVER is a general-domain fact- 
checking dataset created by re-writing Wikipedia sentences 
into atomic claims, verified against Wikipedia articles
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
11
PUBHEALTH gathered public health claims from fact- 
checking websites (eg, Snopes) and verified them against 
news articles with gold-standard labels crafted by journal­
ists
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
26 ManConCorpus is a manually annotated corpus con­
taining pairs of claims and sentences from 259 abstracts 
associated with 24 systematic reviews on cardiovascular dis­
ease
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
27
HealthVer28
focused on COVID-related claims, 
obtained by rewriting answers to questions from TREC- 
COVID29 and validated against the CORD19 corpus
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
30
COVID-Fact scraped COVID-19 claims from a social media 
platform (Reddit) and verified them against linked scientific 
papers and documents retrieved via Google search
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
31 While 
such datasets are essential for developing claim verification 
systems, only some include information regarding study 
design that is key for accurate evidence appraisal
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Therefore, 
existing datasets may not be suitable for verifying clinical- 
related scientific claims since they do not consider the 
strength of studies that produced the evidence
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
To improve 
verification accuracy in the clinical domain, we introduce a 
new Covid VERification dataset (CoVERt)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
It consists of 15 
claims, 96 abstracts with “support” or “refute” labels, and a 
set of sentences extracted from the abstracts containing 
rationales for claim verification
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The claims were encoded 
using the PICO framework (Population, Intervention, 
Comparison, and Outcome), a widely-used knowledge repre­
sentation for clinical questions
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
32 This deliberate design 
choice of CoVERt facilitates seamless alignment with 
extracted evidence from RCTs, thereby providing a founda­
tion for utilizing these rationales in the evaluation process
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We propose an automated PICO-based Claim VERifica­
tion framework (CliVER) that employs retrieval-augmented 
methodologies to verify claims and identify supporting and 
refuting clinical evidence from the clinical trial literature
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We 
also introduce an ensemble of three state-of-the-art systems 
for label prediction
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The outputs of the three systems are 
combined using majority voting
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Evaluations of two testing 
datasets showcase the generalizability of our framework
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We 
observed that the ensemble of two deep language models and 
one prompt-based model improves the verification accuracy 
of each model by an absolute increase in the F1 score from 
3% to 11% on CoVERt
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We compared CliVER’s output for 
19 scientific claims from 6 disease categories to clinicians’ 
manual review
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Our experiment revealed that CliVER 
achieved high accuracy in both the Oracle and Open settings
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Methods
Task formulation
Given a scientific claim c, we retrieved all clinical trial litera­
ture A in PubMed relevant to c
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Following previous stud­
ies,11–13,15 for any given publication, its agreement with the 
claim was represented by one of three labels: Support, Refute, 
and Neutral (neither support nor refute)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For this study, we 
evaluated the PubMed title and abstract for publications 
either with “randomized controlled trial” labeled as publica­
tion type or as Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) term
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
In 
addition, for each abstract a 2 A, we retrieved rationale sen­
tences S 2 a that justify each relationship
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
It is worth noting 
that, to simplify the rational annotation and extraction, our 
gold standard annotations contained a single-sentence ration­
ale
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Therefore, we only required the model to predict one 
rationale sentence
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
CliVER: Baseline model
We developed CliVER with four modules (Figure 1)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Using the PMIDs, we downloaded a total of 189 648 
clinical trial publications
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For each publication, the title, 
abstract, and metadata (eg, authors, journal title, and publi­
cation date) were parsed and stored in JSON files
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We created 
both lexical and dense indexes to enable efficient retrieval
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Lexical indexes were built with Lucene to store term-to- 
document mappings for fast response to “bag of words” 
querying
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Dense indexes were FAISS indexes built upon 
document-level vectors encoded with the pre-trained Col­
BERT model
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
21 Faiss supports efficient similarity search in 
sets of dense vectors
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Document retrieval
Given a scientific claim, this module retrieved the top-k clini­
cal trial publications using a hybrid approach
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
A claim is

Then, we employed two similarity search­
ers, LuceneSearcher and FaissSearcher, to retrieve the n most 
relevant candidate articles to the claim
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
LuceneSearcher eval­
uates claim-article similarity using bag-of-words representa­
tions, while FaissSearcher uses dense transformer-derived 
representations
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The articles identified by two searchers are 
ranked by the relevance score of a candidate article to the 
claim, with a pre-trained document rank model (T5 
ranker33)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The top-k articles were returned with respect to 
the estimated scores
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Sentence selection
Given the claim and each of the top-k articles, this module 
selects a top-t rationale sentence(s) from the abstract
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For 
simplicity, we set t ¼1 to select one sentence with the highest 
similarity score as the primary rationale
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
To achieve this, we 
used the same document ranking model (T5 ranker) to rank 
similarities between the claim’s and each sentence’s 
embeddings
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Label prediction
Given the claim, the abstract, and their candidate rationale 
sentences, this module predicts the label of the abstract
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We 
treated it as a multiclass classification problem, where the 
output indicates whether a given rationale sentence Supports, 
(is) Neutral, or Refutes a claim
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We hypothesized that an ensemble system combining the 
results of different methods could yield better predictive per­
formance than a single method
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
34,35 Hence, we addressed the 
label prediction task using an ensemble system that combined 
the results from three individual models (Figure 1, module 4)
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Each individual system included two BERT-based models 
and one prompt-based model
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For the BERT-based approach, we concatenated a claim c 
and its corresponding sentence s with the special token [SEP] 
and added a prefix token [CLS] to form an input sequence 
[CLS, c, SEP, s], and fed this input to BERT
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
The model takes 
a sequence of tokens with a maximum length of 128 and pro­
duces a 768-dimensional sequence representation vector
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For 
text shorter than 128 tokens, we added paddings (empty 
tokens) to the end of the text to make up the length
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
For text 
that is longer than 128 tokens, we used the first 128 tokens 
as the input
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Then, a fully connected layer is appended on top 
of the [CLS] representation
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
Finally, a softmax layer maps the 
representation vector to the target label space
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We trained the 
model with the objective function set to minimize cross- 
entropy loss between the predicted and manually annotated 
labels
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
We selected RoBERTa36 and PubMedBERT37 as our 
backbone models
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
RoBERTa was chosen because it demon­
strated strong performance in previous work VERISCI
paper_name: Retrieval augmented scientific claim verification

text:
In this paper, we introduce Valsci, an open-source, self-hostable utility 
that automates large-batch scientific claim verification using any OpenAI-compatible 
large language model
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci unites retrieval-augmented generation with structured 
bibliometric scoring and chain-of-thought prompting, enabling users to efficiently 
search, evaluate, and summarize evidence from the Semantic Scholar database 
and other academic sources
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Unlike conventional standalone LLMs, which often suffer 
from hallucinations and unreliable citations, Valsci grounds its analyses in verifiable 
published findings
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
A guided prompt-flow approach is employed to generate query 
expansions, retrieve relevant excerpts, and synthesize coherent, evidence-based 
reports
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Results:  Preliminary evaluations across claims from the SciFact benchmark dataset 
reveal that Valsci significantly outperforms base GPT-4o outputs in citation hallucina-
tion rate while maintaining a low misclassification rate
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The system is highly scalable, 
processing hundreds of claims per hour through asynchronous parallelization
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Conclusions:  By providing an open and transparent platform for large-batch literature 
verification, Valsci substantially lowers the barrier to comprehensive evidence-based 
reviews and fosters a more reproducible research ecosystem
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Researchers, particularly in fields such as bioinformatics and biomedical sciences, 
routinely confront the challenge of rapidly verifying new hypotheses or large sets of

text:
Classical approaches to literature review require manu-
ally searching, extracting, and synthesizing evidence, and can be slow, labor-intensive, 
and prone to human error [2]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The urgency of generating accurate, real-time insights has 
thus sparked intense interest in automating, scaling, and streamlining the claim verifica-
tion process
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Scientific claim verification—the specific task of assessing the accuracy and validity 
of statements based on evidence extracted from existing literature—is central to this 
process of literature review, and therefore to advancing knowledge while safeguard-
ing research quality and reproducibility
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Its importance lies in preventing the spread of 
misinformation, enhancing trust in published findings, and enabling rapid, evidence-
informed decision-making in research and clinical settings
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The rise of AI‑assisted literature analysis & current gaps
Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have transformed various 
domains of natural language processing (NLP), from machine translation to summari-
zation
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Yet, when deployed as stand-alone tools for scientific claim verification, LLMs 
often struggle with hallucinations—fabricating data or citing non-existent sources—
thereby undermining their utility as reliable evidence synthesizers [6]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Many commercial 
platforms, such as Scite [10], Elicit, and Consensus, have integrated NLP-based capa-
bilities to expedite literature retrieval and summarization
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
However, these proprietary 
solutions typically operate as “black boxes” and impose financial or usage constraints, 
making them suboptimal for large-scale or highly specialized academic applications
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Existing open-source tools have made important strides toward transparent, repro-
ducible literature analysis
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
For instance, some employ retrieval-augmented generation 
(RAG) to ensure that LLM outputs are grounded in texts scraped from academic reposi-
tories [8]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Others provide user-friendly environments to rank or cluster abstracts based 
on relevance to predefined queries
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Yet, most of these frameworks either lack end-to-
end pipelines—requiring users to manually feed abstracts—or cannot efficiently handle 
large numbers of claims concurrently
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
As a result, researchers aiming for high-through-
put verification continue to face a patchwork of partial solutions
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci: an end‑to‑end solution
In response to these limitations, we present Valsci, an open-source and fully self-hosta-
ble utility for automated scientific claim verification at scale
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The source code for Valsci 
is distributed under the June 29, 2007 Version 3 GNU General Public License
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
•	 Structured Bibliometric Scoring: In addition to relevance, Valsci incorporates an 
author’s H-index [5], citation counts, and estimated journal impact into an overall 
evidence score, providing a more nuanced view of source credibility
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
This approach mitigates the 
risk of hallucinations by requiring the LLM to cite and assess real, validated excerpts
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
•	 High-Throughput, Asynchronous Execution: Designed for large-batch tasks, Valsci 
employs asynchronous parallelization in Python to manage network requests, token 
usage, and inference calls concurrently
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
With typical resource settings, the system 
can process hundreds of claims per hour—far outpacing both manual human review-
ers and single-threaded open-source tools
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Contributions and significance
This work aims to advance automated literature verification by offering a robust, trans-
parent, and scalable alternative to proprietary solutions
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
We show that Valsci not only 
lowers the misclassification rate for scientific claims compared to direct outputs from 
base language models, but also successfully eliminates citation hallucinations
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Addi-
tionally, it offers a speed increase of over thirty times under reasonable API rate limits 
compared to manual review by undergraduate researchers
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
By coupling an open-source, 
modular design with flexible integration options for LLM backends, Valsci empow-
ers researchers to streamline large-scale systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and high-
throughput validation of computational predictions
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Related work
The growing complexity and volume of scientific literature have driven the develop-
ment of AI-assisted tools for claim verification, literature review, and citation analysis
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
While many systems now integrate large language models (LLMs) with bibliographic 
databases, existing approaches remain constrained in scalability, cost, transparency, and 
automation
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci differs by being fully open-source, self-hostable, and designed for 
high-throughput, large-batch claim verification
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
It combines retrieval-augmented gen-
eration (RAG) with structured bibliometric scoring and guided chain-of-thought (CoT) 
reasoning [15], offering an end-to-end, adaptable alternative to proprietary and aca-
demic solutions
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
In the NLP community, claim verification is often formalized as a classification or 
natural language inference (NLI) task, in which claims are evaluated against retrieved 
evidence, producing judgments such as supported, refuted, or inconclusive [3, 11]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
State-
of-the-art solutions typically integrate retrieval systems with neural inference models; 
however, comprehensive and scalable end-to-end open-source implementations remain 
limited
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Closed-Source Tools One of the earliest AI-driven citation analysis tools, Scite, clas-
sifies citations as supporting, contradicting, or mentioning a referenced claim from a 
particular academic work
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
By extracting citation context, it provides a useful measure 
of how a study is discussed in the literature
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
However, Scite does not actively retrieve or 
analyze new sources; rather, it depends on existing citation networks
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
This approach lim-
its its utility for claims that require comprehensive evidence synthesis from the broader 
scientific corpus
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text: Unlike Scite, Valsci dynamically searches for relevant literature,
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Page 4 of 25
Edelman and Skolnick ﻿BMC Bioinformatics          (2025) 26:140 
extracts supporting text, and evaluates the claim through structured analysis, ensuring 
that verification is based on the strongest available evidence rather than pre-existing 
citation relationships
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Closed-source LLM-assisted literature review tools such as Elicit and Consensus aim 
to automate evidence retrieval and synthesis but remain black-box systems with unclear 
weighting of retrieved sources
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Elicit allows users to search for relevant literature and 
extract key findings but imposes constraints on how many papers can be processed, with 
a “Pro” plan allowing only 1,200 papers per year
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Consensus, built on the same Semantic 
Scholar database as Valsci, offers a “Pro Analysis” feature that provides structured evi-
dence summaries, though the exact methodology behind its synthesis remains opaque
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text: Both tools are subscription-based, limiting accessibility and transparency
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci, by con-
trast, is both open-source and allows unrestricted batch processing at no cost, making it 
more suitable for large-scale systematic reviews and high-volume research workflows
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Open-Source Tools Unlike these proprietary systems, open-source solutions such as 
LitLLM and LLAssist provide more transparent AI-assisted literature analysis
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
LitLLM 
applies retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ensure that LLM-generated summa-
ries are grounded in retrieved texts, reducing the risk of hallucination [1]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
However, it 
requires users to manually provide abstracts for input, rather than performing active lit-
erature searches
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
LLAssist takes a batch of abstracts and ranks them by relevance to a 
predefined research question but does not automatically search for or retrieve relevant 
papers [4]
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Other open-source solutions include systems such as MultiVerS [14], which focus on 
document-level fact-checking in controlled scientific contexts and rely on specialized 
model training (i
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
resource-intensive, domain-specific fine-tuning) for tasks like anno-
tating SciFact claims
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Consequently, these systems are less generalizable to arbitrary 
domains and lack the end-to-end automation needed for large-scale claim verification 
across diverse scientific fields
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
These tools serve as useful components for systematic review workflows but do not 
provide a fully automated end-to-end pipeline
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci extends beyond retrieval and rank-
ing by autonomously handling search, evaluation, bibliometric scoring, and final synthe-
sis into structured claim verification reports
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Focus on Scalability A critical distinction between Valsci and these alternatives is 
its ability to process large batches of claims efficiently
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Existing retrieval-based systems, 
whether open-source or commercial, generally handle claims sequentially or within 
limited pre-configured batch sizes
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci, leveraging asynchronous parallelization of 
network requests, can simultaneously process as many claims as the throughput of the 
configured LLM providing endpoint supports
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
This scalability is particularly valuable for 
research fields requiring high-throughput verification, such as bioinformatics and clini-
cal guideline assessments, where researchers often need to verify thousands of claims 
derived from computational models
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
While existing AI-driven literature tools have made notable strides, none provide an 
open, self-hostable, end-to-end claim verification system that integrates retrieval, evalu-
ation, bibliometric scoring, and structured synthesis into a single pipeline
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci fills this 
gap by offering an adaptable and scalable solution that is not only more cost-effective but 
also ensures transparency and reproducibility in AI-assisted scientific verification
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:

Implementation
The claim verification process begins with query generation, where an LLM is 
employed to transform a claim into multiple well-structured search queries opti-
mized for literature retrieval
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Instead of relying on simple keyword extraction, Valsci 
employs a strategic query expansion prompt designed to maximize the likelihood that 
relevant literature entries are captured by the search
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
This involves incorporating syn-
onyms, alternative phrasing, and mechanistic decompositions to ensure a diverse set 
of relevant papers is retrieved
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Additionally, the system deliberately constructs que-
ries that target both supporting and refuting evidence, ensuring that the retrieved lit-
erature provides a balanced assessment of the claim
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Queries are tailored to match the 
search conventions of Semantic Scholar and other academic search engines, improv-
ing retrieval efficiency across multiple disciplines
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The prompt used to perform this 
expansion is available in Appendix A
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The information flow through Valsci’s modular 
data processing structure is depicted in Fig
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Once queries are generated, Valsci executes them against the free Semantic Scholar 
Search API to obtain a corpus of potentially relevant papers
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The response from the 
API includes the unique identifier of academic works in the Semantic Scholar Data-
base, which is locally hosted within Valsci for fast retrieval without additional API 
calls
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Valsci includes a utility allowing the user to download the full Semantic Scholar 
database as part of the system setup
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The pattern of leveraging the Semantic Scholar 
API for relevance search in combination with Valsci’s indexing utility for fast retrieval 
of texts from the local database is what makes effective retrieval augmented genera-
tion (RAG) against such a large corpus of data possible
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

Information goes through 
the stages of retrieval (blue), analysis (orange), and synthesis (green)
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

For each claim, up to N-times-M papers 
may be evaluated
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

To maximize access to full-text sources, the system first attempts to retrieve the work 
from the local Semantic Scholar Open Research Corpus (S2ORC), which contains full-
text versions of millions of academic papers
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
By prioritizing full-text retrieval, Valsci 
increases the likelihood of retrieving detailed excerpts rather than relying solely on 
abstracts
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
However, to simultaneously ensure that the breadth of literature coverage is 
not unnecessarily limited, abstracts are still used as a fallback if the full text is not avail-
able
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The retrieval engine employs deduplication techniques through an LLM call to 
prevent redundant papers from being analyzed multiple times
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
With default settings of 
five generated queries and a cap of up to five papers retrieved per query, the standard 
retrieval process typically yields between 10 and 20 papers per claim, although this con-
figuration is adjustable based on computational resources and research requirements
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Following retrieval, each paper undergoes content extraction and relevance analysis 
to identify key excerpts that pertain to the claim under investigation
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Using a structured 
LLM prompt, Valsci isolates directly relevant statements, mechanistic explanations, 
and empirical findings that either support or contradict the claim
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
These excerpts are 
extracted verbatim, preserving critical context, including statistical findings and meth-
odological details
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The verbatim excerpts are also paired with an explanation to provide 
relevant context or rationale for the excerpt’s inclusion in the final analysis
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The rel-
evance of each excerpt is then assessed using a confidence score ranging from 0 to 1, 
ensuring that only evidence that could reasonably be considered potentially relevant is 
considered in later synthesis stages
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
If a paper is determined to have no relevance, it is 
classified separately and excluded from final synthesis
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
To further refine the claim verification process, Valsci implements an optional evi-
dence-scoring mechanism that ranks papers based on their scientific credibility and 
impact
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
Unlike simple relevance-based ranking, this scoring process incorporates bib-
liometric indicators such as the H-index of the first and last authors, the citation count 
of the paper, and an LLM-derived estimate of the journal’s impact
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The author impact 
metric considers the expertise of the researchers involved, while citation count provides 
a measure of how influential the study has been in the field
paper_name: Valsci an open‑source, self‑hostable

text:
The journal impact metric 
is assessed using an LLM prompt aiming to recognize venue prestige based on historical 
publication standards and citation patterns in the model’s parametric knowledge, which 
simplifies implementation by removing the need for integration with an external data-
base
